analogy: Think of "Political Theory an Introduction" through the chapter's own opening images: IntroductionPolitical Theory 2 Political Theory Human beings are unique in two respects: they possess reason and the ability to reflect on their actions. They also have the capacity to use language and communicate with each other. Unlike other species, they can express their innermost thoughts... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Political Theory an Introduction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that introductionPolitical Theory 2 Political Theory Human beings are unique in two respects: they possess reason and the ability to reflect on their actions.

The text explains that they also have the capacity to use language and communicate with each other.

The text explains that unlike other species, they can express their innermost thoughts and desires; they can share their ideas and discuss what they consider to be good and desirable.

The text explains that political theory has its roots in the twin aspects of the human self.

The text explains that it analyses certain basic questions such as how should society be organised?

The text explains that political theory examines questions of this kind and systematically thinks about the values that inform political life — values such as freedom, equality and justice.

The text explains that it explains the meaning and significance of these and other related concepts.

The text explains that it clarifies the existing definitions of these concepts by focusing on some major political thinkers of the past and present.

The text explains that it also examines the extent to which freedom or equality are actually present in the institutions that we participate in, everyday life such as schools, shops, buses or trains or government offices.

The text explains that at an advanced level, it looks at whether existing definitions are adequate and how existing institutions (government, bureaucracy) and policy practices must be modified to become more democratic.

The text explains that the objective of political theory is to train citizens to think rationally about political questions and assess the political events of our time.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will examine what is meant by politics and political theory and why we should study it.

The text explains that you would have noticed that people have different ideas about what politics is.

The text explains that political leaders, and persons who contest elections and hold political office, may argue that it is a kind of public service.

The text explains that some others associate politics with manipulation and intrigue undertaken to pursue ambitions and satisfy wants.

The text explains that a few think of politics as what politicians do.

The text explains that these ideas find their place in the Indian Constitution; our Preamble enshrines freedom and equality; the chapter on Rights in the Indian Constitution abolishes untouchability in any form; Gandhian principles find a place in Directive Principles.

The text explains that we must be enabled to go to school or get help through proactive measures such as fair distribution of resources (jobs, decent wages, subsidised hospitals, etc.).

The text explains that as high school students, we may choose one of the above professions in the future and so indirectly it is relevant for us even now.

The text explains that which of the following statements are true/false about Political Theory?

The text explains that (a) It discusses ideas that form the basis of political institutions.

The text explains that (b) It explains the relationship between different religions.

The text explains that (c) It explains the meanings of concepts like equality and freedom.

The text explains that (d) It predicts the performance of political parties.

The text explains that vigilant citizens are a must for the successful working of a democracy.

The text explains that in what ways is the study of political theory useful for us?

The text explains that identify four ways in which political theory can be useful to us?

The text explains that do you think that a good/convincing argument can compel others to listen to you?

The text explains that do you think studying political theory is like studying mathematics?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 1: Political Theory an Introduction

cases.name: Mahatma Gandhi
cases.point: Mahatma Gandhi once observed that politics envelops us like the coils of a snake and there is no other way out but to wrestle with it. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 2)

cases.name: IntroductionPolitical Theory
cases.point: IntroductionPolitical Theory 4 Political Theory are formed and how they function is thus an important focus of politics. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 3)

cases.point: These documents did not just emerge overnight; they are built upon the ideas and principles debated almost since the time of Kautilya, Aristotle to Jean Jacques... (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 4)

cases.name: Karl Marx
cases.point: Karl Marx argued that equality was as crucial as freedom. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 4)

schemes.point: No society can exist without some form of political organisation and collective decision making. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 2)

schemes.name: We
schemes.point: We see that governments determine our economic policy and foreign policy and educational policy. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1.1 WHAT IS POLITICS? and LET’S DEBATE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Political Theory an Introduction; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Political Theory an Introduction through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1.1 WHAT IS POLITICS? and LET’S DEBATE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
